title: Create a Vec
successMessage: vec! is the quick constructor. Vec::new() + push builds incrementally. Both produce the same type.

title: Access elements
instruction:
Index with `[]` (panics if out of bounds) or `.get()` (returns Option).

Run this.
successMessage: v[2] panics on out-of-bounds. v.get(2) returns Option — safe for user input or dynamic indices.

title: Iterate a Vec
instruction:
`for x in &v` iterates by reference. `for x in v` consumes the Vec.

Run this.
successMessage: &v borrows — v is still usable after the loop. Without & the loop would consume v.

title: push and pop
instruction:
`push` adds to the end. `pop` removes the last element and returns Option.

Run this.
successMessage: while let Some(x) = v.pop() drains the vec from the end. Pop returns None when empty, ending the loop.

title: filter and map
instruction:
`.iter().filter().map().collect()` transforms a Vec without a loop.

Run this.
successMessage: filter keeps elements matching the predicate. map transforms each. collect turns the iterator back into a Vec.

title: sort and dedup
instruction:
`sort()` sorts in place. `dedup()` removes consecutive duplicates.

Run this.
successMessage: Sort first, then dedup removes consecutive duplicates. Together they remove all duplicates from the vec.

title: len, is_empty, contains
successMessage: iter().sum() uses a turbofish ::<i32> to tell Rust what type to sum into. Common pattern when type inference needs a hint.

title: Vec of structs
instruction:
Vecs can hold any type, including your custom structs.

Run this.
successMessage: sort_by_key sorts by a field. The closure extracts the key. This is a clean, idiomatic pattern for sorting collections of structs.
